title: Find the Highest Altitude
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal challenge: the single recommended solution is also the speed extreme and the memory extreme. Per the repo standard (no coincident files), only solution.cpp is shipped; there is no separate solution-runtime or solution-memory file.

variants.languageReason: The problem is a tiny arithmetic-only prefix scan. Native C++ uses LeetCode's vector<int>& input without copying, avoids interpreter and managed-runtime overhead, and keeps the method allocation-free with two scalar integers.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected for native execution, zero-copy vector<int>& judge input, compact integer arithmetic, and official class-based LeetCode ergonomics.
Native and similarly compact, but it does not reduce the algorithmic state below two integers or provide a meaningful memory advantage over the C++ signature for this problem.
Competitive native performance, but safety/bounds-check codegen and submission overhead do not improve on the simple C++ scalar loop.
A slice loop is straightforward, but Go runtime and bounds-check overhead add no advantage for n <= 100.
JIT performance is adequate, but managed runtime overhead and metadata are unnecessary for a tiny one-pass arithmetic method.
The constraints are small enough for acceptance, but interpreter or VM overhead is much larger than the work done by the algorithm.

variants.approachJustification: Maintain the current altitude as a running prefix sum and update the best altitude after each gain. The starting altitude 0 initializes the maximum. This same scan is also the speed and memory extreme.

reasoningSummary: The altitude at each point is the prefix sum of gains up to that point. Because the answer only needs the maximum prefix value and every gain may change it, a single running altitude plus a running maximum is both time-optimal and memory-optimal.
